item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: threat to normalcy? everyone on the planet died!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this ones actually really cool to read. i like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No go with "The situation isn't bad enough to utilize SCP-2000". Using it now would be a threat to normalcy, even if said normalcy now revolves around weird magic people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly didn't think of that👎

SCP-2000 was never invented in this timeline👎

The machine's biomatter reserves were infected with blue matter and mutant super-clones swarmed out when they turned it on.👍

Thank you for reading!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the end, in Procedure 99-Lilac, they mention using E-Class survivors to repopulate the Earth. Couldn’t they use SCP-2000 instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No monumental evil act in the history of mankind has been committed by anyone who thought of themselves as "evil" - on the contrary, the worse the (objective) evil, the more the perpetrator was completely convinced of the goodness of himself and of his "purification".

— Eric Naggum

+1

item.title: Re: TFW you were the doomsday all along
item.content:encoded: Ok that came out really well. I like how they casually drop any ethics department regs at the first sign of trouble. Shows the state of moral decay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here I am first reading this on 10/17/18, making me suspicious of the timeline starting on 10/16. Didn't see any funny business in the source code though so just a coincidence I guess!

Anyway, I did have a couple questions though: how big are the giant robots? There's nothing in the picture to give a clear sense of scale, so it could be anywhere from the size of a person to the size of an office building.

Second question is about this line in the description: "the number of living SCP-3731 instances is no more than 5% of the baseline human population." The word "baseline" is what's confusing me. Is it saying that "5% of [all humans that would be alive if 3396 hadn't showed up] are mutants and 95% are either dead or in the orange zone"?

All in all though, I really enjoyed this series. Satisfying conclusion and unlikely to give me nightmares.

item.title: Re: TFW you were the doomsday all along
item.content:encoded: I helped field out with the CSS and editing on this, and he's graciously offered to list me as a contributor. I just want to use this comment to make it clear that Field absolutely deserves full credit for the excellent content on display here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great article - tense tone and apocalyptic story progression mixed with a bit of science fiction. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was extremely well-done. I'm a little biased because I really like that it builds on the developing "SCPverse magic system" I've contributed to over the years, but I would have upvoted anyway. Exactly the amount of cool details I want from something like this. (Also, "residual haunting" was fantastic.) Just really excellent all-around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Field did a great job with the logs. Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good stuff. I was already at +1 before this but by this line I'd have upvoted twice if I could:

May cause residual haunting.

There's so much to like here, but my favourite is all the small little details thrown in there about the the state of life outside of the orange zone (sentient lightning storms is a very cool image). It manages to to maintain consistency throughout the logs without becoming ridiculous or feeling forced, and manages to incorporate huge scale loss of life in a way that isn't gauche.

Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Define 'humans'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I almost downvoted this by reflex, as if I would be downvoting the Foundation and not, you know, a story.

I didn't, though, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it boss!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really appreciate this comment. The Foundation make terrifying villains, and we knew that's what they would be here, but it was important that they have logical reasons for what they do.

+ Let's go way too deeply into that
– hide block

I thought a lot while I was writing this about how regular, well-intentioned people end up doing horrible things. Everything the Foundation does here would seem justifiable if backed by unwavering belief in two simple premises: One, "human life takes precedence over other kinds;" and two, "anomalies aren't human." I wanted to show how, as desperation mounts, the inability of the leaders to compromise ruins them, but even in the end they're not evil for evil's sake. They're acting out of a belief that what they're doing is right. The capacity of people to do wrong things for seemingly right reasons is a source of real fear for me, so this was a great opportunity to tap into it.

As my team worked together and egged each other on, an aesthetic of "everything larger than life, fireworks from beginning to end" emerged. As this portrayal of the Foundation gradually became the badass magitech engine of doom it ended up, I worked hard to balance that with the more serious perspective on how villains are really made without making their behavior a caricature (or even worse, glorifying it).

There were points where I felt really uncomfortable with the whole concept and started to think it was a bad idea. Comments like this make me feel like the themes came across like I was hoping they would, which makes it all worthwhile.

I'm overjoyed that people felt the intense disgust that I was going for, because disgust keeps us safe from things that would contaminate us. That's a healthy reaction to the rigidity of the overseers in this, and the complacence of those who follow them. People are being locked up and dehumanized in my corner of the world even as I write this. The idea that my dumb story might provoke real thought about how that happens means a lot to me.

Also, every word of that technobabble was agonizingly thought out, so I'm glad you noticed 😀

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig this.

As things continue to head further and further south for the Foundation you can really see the desperation in their actions, which makes this article a particularly compelling read as well as making me really want to jump right into the finale.

+1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the only ways in which a totalitarian (right word, I think?), militaristic reading of the Foundation works for me, and you go through great pains to make sure how fucked up the Foundation gets to achieve their goal as well. The jargon works well here, because it supports the article rather than being its core.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Ngl for a minute by brain was parsing that as "Scorche Death" and I was very confused XD

Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That headcannon bit definitely should have gone in there somewhere.

Expanding on the "residual haunting" and such.

The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man
The Ascension of the Ordinary Man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, the weapons are bad ass.

Also, the Foundation doing genocide is always intriguing, though not original1, since it really highlights the coldness of what they will do. Also, to clarify, the Foundation are the only humans left?

My only complaint about this article is the color of the document. The purple background makes my eyes a bit sore when staring at it as I read the text.

Overall, I give my upvote.

Footnotes
1. I think I recall the Foundation killing an entire species of whales, and heavily decrease the numbers of a few species of SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly the kind of feel our team has been aiming for, so it's great to see that it's coming across!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting to contrast this with some of the other contest entries- elsewhere, when the Foundation has been forced into an 'adapt or die' situation, they've chosen to adapt- so they just kind of adjust their definition of normal to include the presence of bee people or the absence of death.

But here they've instead chosen to stubbornly dig in their heels and continue to fight, until it turns out that the initial anomaly was a red herring, and the world is actually going to be destroyed by the increasingly fanatical and monstrous remnants of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Causes large holes to open on surfaces within the area of detonation; groups of elongated, multi-jointed human limbs emerge from holes to detect sapient organisms and pull them inside, after which the holes demanifest. Effective for depopulating SCP-3731 instances while minimizing damage to infrastructure.

I had to upvote right at this moment. The simple image of the Foundation using this weapons is twisted as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s obviously just SCP-131 in its own mecha :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, this is high praise. My team were very tough acts to follow.

Also, that's not an eye on the robot, it's a laser that shoots you so hard it kills your parents before they met and prevents you from ever being there. It just looks like an iris while it's charging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, there's no guarantee that conventional bombing would kill all the mutants- some of them might even get stronger. Only the most terrible soul ripping badass superweapons can get this job done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really excited that you like this, since I've always like your action-paratech stories. Here's to throwing suspension of disbelief aside and making a mad dash of awesomeness instead.

The way they keep developing better countermeasures even as everything falls apart is pretty improbable, but one explanation is

+ spoilerish author headcanon
– hide block

O5 mandate 3731-Ov76 (7 in favor, 4 opposed, 2 abstaining) authorizes use of anomalous methods in the containment of SCP-3731, including reverse engineering of paratechnology, use of necrothaumaturgy, and collusion with extradimensional entities.

I'm not saying the Foundation made a literal deal with the devil/Scarlet King/whatever for the ability to combat the mutants… but I wouldn't put it past them either.

I did want to put in that after starting with the Lilac procedures, they start getting reports from within the Zone of "screams with no identifiable source and independent action by shadows and reflections (personnel are strongly recommended not to interact with autonomous reflections)," but couldn't find room.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This features three things I always love in skips: Specific containment procedures with meaningful technobabble, in-depth worldbuilding through use of logs, and the Foundation doing fucked up shit. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they nuked them that close to the orange zone, they’d cover part of their only safe area in fallout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do have one question, though. Cant the foundation just bomb New Vegas and Monica back to the stone age?

item.title: Re: Clinical Containment Procedures
item.content:encoded: +1 this time/

item.title: Clinical Containment Procedures
item.content:encoded: This is actually what i envisioned Foundation response to the situation would be like. i applaud you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Manifests spectral activity in a directed stream in a manner analogous to a flamethrower. May cause residual haunting.

Badass.

Also, speaking of haunting, that final timeline (in "Early Encounters") event hurt my soul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo! This is one of the best scips I've read in a long time. +1 you brilliant psychopaths

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the errors and added in our crosslinks, thanks for the spot ihp!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a few issues I've spotted in here— You say "05" and "O5" interchangeably in a few points, and a few of your nested quotations seem to be broken. Get rid of the space between the two ">" symbols and that should fix it.

It's pretty good outside of the formatting issues, though. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"oh my God, you clinical assholes! they're humans!"

"Not according to my paperwork."

Seriously, thanks for reading! I'm glad this gave you a strong reaction. Fortunately, it's not over yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this was a gripping read. At first I was totally against the Foundation's reaction to the mutations, but the fact that the world would become uninhabitable was a gut punch. I don't agree with their measures, but damn if they weren't trying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i wonder how many times i silently shouted, "oh my God, you clinical assholes! they're humans!"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh shit.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the exact sort of over-the-top insanity I've come to expect from this team, and it only keeps getting better. I do love the inversion behind the apotheosis scenario. My only gripe is that the iris on the drone seems a bit silly, but that's barely an issue.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic work field!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is now my new favorite anime. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic article. The paratech is fun to envision and chunky to read without being dry, while the escalation of humanity and the Foundation's reaction to it - both inside and out - are well-paced and managed to hold my attention throughout. The ending is also extremely clever, maintaining the original theme of apotheosis while putting an ironic twist on it. My only real gripe is that it seems improbable for the Foundation to keep being able to develop increasingly advanced paratech even though their resource base has dwindled to the southwestern United States.

item.title: TFW you were the doomsday all along
item.content:encoded:
"Life, the way it truly is, is a battle not between good and bad but between bad and worse." - Joseph Brodsky

I hope you enjoy this, the penultimate chapter in team Clever Gentlemen's ludicrous over the top mutant wizard rampage.

Extreme thank you to formatting genius Modern_Erasmus, creative juggernaut CadaverCommander, and fearless leader Weryllium for all the help and for generally being the best goddamn team around. Huge thanks also to the very gentlemanly Captain Kirby (good luck in the contest!) and to GibberingEloquence for helping out with great advice.

Also, thank you to our community outreach team for organizing this madness. Stay tuned for the thrilling conclusion sometime in the next 2 days!

In the meantime, check out my author page for more a bunch more nightmares and a fairy tale or two.

+ image credits
– hide block

Awesome giant robot is CC0 from Pixabay here. Courtesy of Rani_Ramli, who was very cool to put such impressive work out for open use.

"Orange zone" map from wikimedia commons here.

Desert tents from wikimedia commons
here.
